title: Word Part Corners: Prefix Trap Round
subject: literacy_intervention

room_setup: Post one response option in each corner.
Clear a movement path before starting.
reset_notes: Collect corner labels and optional whiteboards at the end.

launch: You will hear or see a word-part prompt. Move to the corner that makes a real word.
Some rounds have one correct corner, some have more than one, and some have none. Be ready to defend your choice.
between_rounds: Freeze. Check the room. Talk to one person near you about why this choice fits.
If you think there is more than one answer, hold your ground and be ready to explain it.
debrief: Which prefixes were easiest to trust, and which ones tricked you?

student_directions: Listen to the prompt and choose the corner that makes a real word.
Some rounds may have more than one answer or no answer.
Be ready to explain why your corner fits.

round_sequence.label: Round 1
round_sequence.teacher_move: Start with a single-correct confidence builder.

round_sequence.label: Round 2
round_sequence.teacher_move: Run a core round with visible word logic.

round_sequence.label: Round 3
round_sequence.teacher_move: Shift into a meaning decision with a stronger abstract word.

debrief_questions: Which round was the biggest trap?
What clue helped you know a word was real or not real?

whiteboard.label: Whiteboard version
notes: Students stay seated and write their chosen response instead of moving.
Use show-and-hide whiteboard reveals for none-correct rounds.
prompt_adjustments: Shorten the wait time between reveal and discussion.

easier.label: Easier variation
notes: Use only starter and single-correct rounds.
Keep one response option as obviously unrelated.
prompt_adjustments: Preteach the prefixes on the board before the first round.

harder.label: Harder variation
notes: Ask students to give a second valid example before scoring the point.
prompt_adjustments: Swap in stretch items from the same bank.

worksheet_companion.label: Companion sheet
notes: Students record the round, their answer, and a short why-it-fits note after each turn.
prompt_adjustments: Use after the movement rounds, not during them.

extension_questions: Which trap round fooled the most people, and why?
What made one prefix feel plausible even when it did not make a real word?

exit_slip.prompt: Use one of today’s prefixes in a real word and explain the meaning signal.

teacher_notes: This routine works best when rounds alternate between confidence-builders and traps.
Keep the pace fast and the explanations short until the final debrief.
differentiation_notes: Use easier rounds with visible stems for students who need a clearer entry point.
Use whiteboard mode when movement would overwhelm the group or space is limited.
curricular_links: Morphology awareness supports decoding, spelling, and vocabulary growth in Grade 6-8 literacy intervention.
